1: 'After this, Absalom son of David had a beautiful sister (ʾāḥôṯ yapâ) named Tamar, and Amnon son of David fell in love with her (wayeʾĕhāḇehā ʾamnôn).' The chapter opens with family relationships: Absalom and Tamar share the same mother; Amnon is Tamar's half-brother, David's firstborn by a different wife. The use of ʾāhēḇ (love) for Amnon's feeling is immediately qualified by the narrative — what follows will show this was not covenant love but consuming desire. The beauty of Tamar and the wrongness of Amnon's desire are juxtaposed in v1's opening.
2: 'Amnon was so tormented (wayyiṣṣer ləʾamnôn) by his desire for Tamar that he became ill (wayyiḥleh), for she was a virgin (bəṯûlâ) and it seemed impossible (wayyibbāṣēr ḇəʿênê ʾamnôn) for him to do anything to her.' The verb ṣārar (to be constrained/distressed) describes Amnon's psychological state. The key term bəṯûlâ (virgin) establishes Tamar's legal status — a virgin daughter under her father's protection, accessible through no normal channel. wayyibbāṣēr (it was withheld/impossible) from bāṣar (to cut off, restrain): the legitimate path to Tamar was cut off.
3: 'But Amnon had a friend named Jonadab, son of Shimeah, David's brother; and Jonadab was a very shrewd man (ḥākām məʾōd).' The description of Jonadab as ḥākām (wise/shrewd) is ironic: he is clever in the service of wickedness — the opposite of the wisdom of Proverbs. His ḥokmâ is ḥokmâ rāʿāh (evil wisdom), the cunning that finds ways to accomplish what ought not to be done.
4: 'He said to Amnon: Why are you, the king's son, growing thinner (dallôṯ) day after day? Won't you tell me?' The term dallôṯ (thin/declining, from dālal to be low/reduced) describes physical deterioration from emotional distress. Jonadab's question is sympathetic but his purpose is to enable sin. His identity as David's nephew (Shimeah is David's brother) means this scheme involves the extended royal family in the conspiracy.
5: 'Jonadab said to him: Lie down on your bed and pretend to be ill. When your father comes to visit you, say to him, Let my sister Tamar come and give me food to eat, and prepare the food in my sight, that I may see it and eat it from her hand.' Jonadab's scheme uses the conventions of illness-hospitality and family care to create access to Tamar. The request mittādehā (from her hand) specifies personal delivery — the detail that will isolate Tamar in Amnon's presence.
6: 'So Amnon lay down and pretended to be ill. And when the king came to see him, Amnon said to the king, Please let my sister Tamar come and make a couple of cakes (šətê lḇiḇōṯ) in my sight, that I may eat from her hand.' The lǝḇiḇāh (cake/heart-shaped bread) appears only here in the OT; its connection to the root lēḇ (heart) may carry the irony that Amnon requests heart-cakes from the woman he claims to love. David's compliance with the request — without apparent suspicion — marks his failure of oversight.
7: 'Then David sent to Tamar's house, saying, Go to your brother Amnon's house and prepare food for him.' David's instruction is the unwitting mechanism of harm. His sending of Tamar to Amnon's house sets the chain in motion. The phrase bêṯ ʾamnôn ʾāḥîḵā (the house of your brother Amnon) — using the familial term — is the cover under which the assault is planned.
8: 'So Tamar went to her brother Amnon's house, where he was lying down. And she took dough and kneaded it and made cakes in his sight and baked the cakes.' Tamar's compliance with the request is complete and innocent — she fulfills the role of caring sister without any knowledge of the scheme. The domestic detail (kneading, baking) grounds the scene in normal household activity, making the sudden assault (v14) more jarring.
9: 'And she took the pan and emptied it out before him, but he refused to eat. And Amnon said, Send out everyone from me. So everyone went out from him.' The refusal to eat is the signal that the scheme was never about food. The clearing of the house (hōṣîʾû kol-ʾîš mēʿālay, send out everyone from me) isolates Tamar. The servants depart without apparent concern — the king's son's request was not unusual to them.
10: 'Then Amnon said to Tamar, Bring the food into the chamber, that I may eat from your hand. And Tamar took the cakes she had made and brought them into the chamber to Amnon her brother.' The movement from the main room to the inner chamber (haḥāḏer) is the last movement before the assault. Tamar follows, still without apparent alarm — the setting remained within the realm of family care.
11: 'But when she brought them near him to eat, he took hold of her (wayeḥĕzeq-bāh) and said to her, Come, lie with me, my sister.' The verb ḥāzaq (to grasp firmly, to overpower) indicates forceful physical restraint — the first moment of explicit coercion. His words use the familial term ʾăḥôṯî (my sister) alongside the demand, which makes the request a double violation: sexual assault of a sibling. Tamar's response in vv12-13 is a composed, reasoned refusal.
12: 'She answered him, No, my brother, do not violate me (ʾal-tənannēnî), for such a thing is not done in Israel (lō-yēʿāśeh ḵēn bəyiśrāʾēl); do not do this outrageous thing (nəḇālâ).' nəḇālâ is the key term: it means an outrage against the social-covenant order, a gross violation of what Israel's communal life requires. The term appears in Genesis 34:7 (Dinah's violation), Deuteronomy 22:21 (sexual immorality), and Judg 19-20 (the Levite's concubine). Tamar names the act with the correct theological and social vocabulary. lō-yēʿāśeh ḵēn bəyiśrāʾēl — not done this way in Israel — invokes the covenant community's standards as the frame for her refusal.
13: 'And I, where would I carry my shame (ʾănî ʾānāh ʾôlîḵ ʾeṯ-ḥerpāṯî)? And as for you, you would be as one of the outrageous fools (kəʾaḥaḏ hannəḇālîm) in Israel. Now therefore, please speak to the king, for he will not withhold me from you.' Tamar's second argument: the social consequences for both. ḥerpâ (shame, disgrace, reproach) — the word used for the permanent social stigma of sexual violation. Her counterproposal — ask the king — indicates she believed a legitimate marriage might be possible (though Leviticus 18:9 and Deuteronomy 27:22 prohibit half-sibling marriage; her suggestion may be desperation or a legal tradition allowing exceptions).
14: 'But he would not listen to her, and being stronger than she, he violated her (wayəḥazzēq mimmennāh wayyiškaḇ ʾōṯāh wayyannahā) and lay with her.' The verb sequence is unambiguous: ḥāzaq (overpowered), šāḵaḇ (lay with), ʿinnāh (violated/humiliated). The verb ʿānāh (Piel: to violate, to humble by force) is the technical term for sexual violence in the OT (Genesis 34:2; Deuteronomy 22:24,29; Ezekiel 22:10-11). The narrative names what happened with legal precision.
15: 'Then Amnon hated her with very great hatred (wayyiśnāʾehā ʾamnôn śinʾāh gəḏôlāh məʾōd), so that the hatred with which he hated her was greater than the love with which he had loved her.' The sentence is one of the most precise psychological observations in the OT. śinʾâ (hatred) is placed against ʾahăḇâ (love) — the verbal contrast exposes what Amnon's 'love' actually was: not covenant commitment but consuming desire that, when fulfilled, turned to loathing. The fulfilled desire without covenant is characterized not by satisfaction but by revulsion — a pattern that distinguishes ʾahăḇâ (covenant love) from ḥāšaq or mere desire.
16: 'And Amnon said to her, Get up! Go (qûmî lēḵî)!' But she said to him, No, my brother, for this wrong in sending me away (hārāʿāh haggəḏôlāh hazzōʾt lǝšalləḥēnî) is greater than the other that you did to me.' Tamar identifies the second act — the dismissal — as a greater wrong than the first. In ANE and Israelite law, a man who violated an unengaged virgin was required to marry her (Deuteronomy 22:28-29) — the dismissal would leave her without legal recourse and in permanent social disgrace. She is naming the legal outcome her rejection creates.
17: 'But he would not listen to her. He called the young man who served him and said, Put this woman out of my presence and bolt the door after her.' haššillîḥāh (send her out) — the same root used for divorce in Deut 24; the association is part of the shame: Amnon treats her as a discarded wife without the legal status. nəʿal haḏdeleṯ ʾaḥărêhā (bolt the door after her) — the locking of the door finalizes the exclusion.
18: 'Now she was wearing a long robe (kəṯōneṯ passîm) with long sleeves, for thus were the virgin daughters of the king dressed.' The kəṯōneṯ passîm is the same garment worn by Joseph (Genesis 37:3) — a distinctive garment marking status. For David's virgin daughters it signified royal virginity and protection. Its tearing in the next verse is both a literal act and a symbolic enactment of what has been taken.
19: 'And Tamar put ashes (ʾēper) on her head and tore the long robe (wattiqraʿ kəṯōneṯ happasîm) that she wore. And she laid her hand on her head and went away, crying aloud as she went.' The mourning ritual Tamar performs — ash on head, torn garment, hand on head, wailing — is the full vocabulary of grief. The torn kəṯōneṯ passîm is particularly significant: the garment of protected virginity is torn, enacting publicly what has happened privately. Her public mourning is an act of witness.
20: šōmēmâ: 'And Tamar lived, a desolate woman (šōmēmâ), in her brother Absalom's house.' The word šōmēmâ (desolate/devastated) is the same term used for the desolation of a land under divine judgment (Isaiah 49:8; Jeremiah 12:11; Lamentations 1:13). Applied to a person, it means the devastation of personhood — the reduction of a full human life to a condition of ruin. Tamar sits desolate in Absalom's house: alive but in a state the text names with the language of national catastrophe. The word choice is the text's theological evaluation of what was done to her.
21: 'When King David heard of all these things, he was very angry (wayyiḥar lô məʾōd), but he would not punish his son Amnon, because he loved him (kî-ʾăhēḇô kî bəḵōrô hûʾ), for he was his firstborn.' David's failure here is judicial: as king, he administered justice to all Israel (2 Samuel 8:15); as father, he failed to administer it within his own household. The stated reason — love of his firstborn — is the same motivation that produced the failure of Eli (1 Samuel 3:13) and of the corrupt judges generally: personal affection overriding judicial obligation. The text offers no mitigation.
22: 'But Absalom spoke to Amnon neither good nor bad, for Absalom hated Amnon (kî-śānēʾ ʾabšālôm ʾeṯ-ʾamnôn) because he had violated his sister Tamar.' Absalom's silence — speaking neither good nor bad — is the silence of a man who has resolved on a course of action and is waiting for the right moment. ʿal-dǝḇar ʿannôṯ ʾeṯ-tāmār ʾăḥōṯô (because of the violation of his sister Tamar) — the stated motivation is the protection of his sister and revenge for the wrong that David failed to address.
23: 'After two full years (šənāyim šānîm) Absalom had sheepshearers at Baal-hazor, which is near Ephraim.' Two years of silence precede the act — the time in which Absalom nursed his hatred (v22) and planned. Baal-hazor is identified with Tell ʿAsur, approximately 15 miles north of Jerusalem, near Ephraim. The sheepshearing festival was a time of feasting and relative security — the occasion Absalom chose for the murder.
24: 'And Absalom came to the king and said, Behold, your servant has sheepshearers. Please let the king and his servants go with your servant.' Absalom's invitation to David is deliberate misdirection — he wants the king's presence to lend legitimacy to the gathering and to gain access to Amnon. David's initial refusal (v25) is the opening for the specific request that follows.
25: 'But the king said to Absalom, No, my son, let us not all go, lest we be burdensome to you. He pressed him, but he would not go and gave him his blessing.' David's reluctance — expressed as concern about the burden of the full court attending — and Absalom's persistent request (wayyiprāṣ-bô, he pressed him) show Absalom's insistence on getting Amnon specifically to the gathering.
26: 'Then Absalom said, If not, please let my brother Amnon go with us.' And the king said to him, Why should he go with you?' Absalom's specific request for Amnon draws David's attention to the unusual nature of the invitation. David's question — why should Amnon specifically go? — suggests some awareness that a targeted request deserves scrutiny, though he ultimately concedes.
27: 'But Absalom pressed him until he let Amnon and all the king's sons go with him.' wayyiprāṣēhû — the same verb as v25 (he pressed/urged insistently). Absalom's persistence overcomes David's hesitation. The presence of all the king's sons protects Absalom from suspicion — it looks like a family gathering, not a targeted assassination.
28: 'Then Absalom commanded his servants, Mark when Amnon's heart is merry with wine (kəṭôḇ lēḇ-ʾamnôn bayyāyin), and when I say to you, Strike Amnon, then kill him. Do not fear; have I not commanded you? Be courageous and be valiant.' kəṭôḇ lēḇ bayyāyin (when the heart is good with wine) — the phrase used for festive inebriation; the same condition Nabal was in when Abigail intervened (1 Samuel 25:36). Absalom waits for the moment of vulnerability. The instruction to his servants — fear not, I command you — frames the murder as an authorized act of justice.
29: 'And the servants of Absalom did to Amnon as Absalom had commanded. Then all the king's sons arose, and each mounted his mule and fled.' The assassination is narrated tersely. The flight of all the king's sons on their mules creates the false report that all the king's sons are dead (v30) — the rapid departure of the group is misread as indicating general slaughter.
30: 'While they were on the way, news came to David, All the king's sons have been killed, and not one of them is left.' The garbled report — all the sons dead — is the misreading of a mass flight. The principle that early reports in crisis are often exaggerated is embedded in the narrative; Jonadab's correction (v32-33) arrives to provide the more accurate account.
31: 'Then the king arose and tore his garments and lay on the ground. And all his servants who were standing by tore their garments.' David's mourning ritual — torn garments, prostration — is the appropriate response to the death-news. The communal mourning of the servants mirrors the king's.
32: 'But Jonadab the son of Shimeah, David's brother, answered, Let not my lord suppose that they have killed all the young men, the king's sons, for Amnon alone is dead. For by the command of Absalom this has been determined from the day he violated his sister Tamar.' Jonadab's clarification is accurate but also incriminating of himself: he knew the plan had been set from 'the day Tamar was violated' — suggesting he had information about Absalom's intent and said nothing. His earlier role in enabling the scheme (v3-5) and his now-demonstrated knowledge of the aftermath put him in an uncomfortable position.
33: 'Now therefore let not my lord the king take it to heart to suppose that all the king's sons are dead, for Amnon alone is dead.' Jonadab's twice-stated correction — only Amnon is dead — is reassurance designed also to manage David's response. His knowledge of the plan's specific target confirms advance information.
34: 'But Absalom fled. And the young man who kept the watch lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold, many people were coming from the road behind him by the side of the mountain.' The watchman's report of people coming down from the hill road allows the king to receive the returning sons before he has fully processed the news.
35: 'And Jonadab said to the king, Behold, the king's sons have come; as your servant said, so it has come about.' Jonadab's vindication of his own report is self-serving: he is positioning himself as a trustworthy counselor even though he had enabled the original crime. His role in the narrative is consistently that of shrewd self-preservation at others' expense.
36: 'And as soon as he had finished speaking, the king's sons came and lifted up their voices and wept. And the king also and all his servants wept very bitterly.' The family grief is genuine: though only Amnon is dead, the event has fractured the household irrevocably. Absalom is now a fugitive; the sword that Nathan decreed (v10) has entered the house.
37: 'But Absalom fled and went to Talmai the son of Ammihud, king of Geshur (wayyāmāṯ ʾeṯ-māʿăḵāh). And David mourned for his son day after day.' Geshur was a small Aramean kingdom northeast of the Sea of Galilee. Absalom's mother Maacah was the daughter of Talmai king of Geshur (2 Samuel 3:3) — Absalom fled to his maternal grandfather. The refuge is both geographically safe and genealogically connected, placing him beyond David's immediate reach.
38: 'So Absalom fled and went to Geshur, and was there three years.' Three years in exile — the same temporal unit that the Gibeonite famine will span (2 Samuel 21:1). The three-year period allows Absalom to develop his estrangement from David into a political strategy. David's inability to act decisively (to punish Amnon, then to recall Absalom) reflects the same paralysis that characterized his response to Tamar's violation.
39: 'And the spirit of the king longed to go out to Absalom (watəḵal nēpeš-haMMeleḵ lāṣēṯ ʾel-ʾabšālôm), because he was comforted about Amnon, since he was dead.' The verb kālâ (to be spent/consumed/finished) with nēpeš (soul/desire) describes David's consuming longing — his desire to go to Absalom had worn him out. The grief for Amnon has reached its end (nāḥam, he was comforted, meaning the acute grief had passed); what remains is the longing for the living son who fled. The tension between justice (Absalom killed his brother) and paternal love drives the next chapter.
